result:
The silence before the typing begins is always the loudest. A blank page, a blinking cursor, a thousand-word void waiting to be filled. It feels less like an empty vessel and more like a wide, flat plain under a pale sky. You can walk in any direction. The only rule is to cover some ground.

It starts with noticing. The weight of my own hands on the keyboard, the faint hum of the computer, a distant siren threading through the afternoon. We are so rarely here, in this exact configuration of atoms, this specific second of our one long, unraveling story. We are already mourning it as it passes. That’s the human trick, and the human tragedy: to live in the already-gone and the not-yet, and to call the ghost between them “now.”

I think about the other people, right now, in their own thousand-word spaces. A woman in Lisbon is staring at a blank email, trying to find the gentle words to end a thing that wasn’t meant to end. A teenager in Kyoto is meticulously crafting a social media post that feels, to them, like etching a declaration on a monument. A man in a Toronto hospital is rehearsing, for the thousandth time in his head, the three sentences he will say to his father when the breathing tube comes out. We are all, constantly, writing our tiny, urgent scriptures. Most are never read aloud.

There’s a cobweb in the upper corner of my window, strung between the pane and the brick. The architect of this delicate, deadly palace is nowhere to be seen. It trembles, a perfect radar net, waiting for the shudder of a life it cannot comprehend, only consume. I am no different. I wait for the tremor of a thought, a feeling, a memory—some bit of life to catch in the web of language. Then I consume it, trying to spin its essence into a pattern someone else might recognize.

Language is such a flawed magic. We take the riot of sensation—the smell of rain on hot concrete, the specific ache of a forgotten dream, the texture of loneliness on a Sunday evening—and we compress it into these blunt, alphabetical tokens. We hand them over, hoping the person on the other side will unpack them and reconstruct, not just the fact, but the *feeling*. It’s a miracle it ever works. Most of the time, we are just ghosts humming to other ghosts through a thick wall.

Maybe that’s why we tell stories. Not to explain, but to build a shared room inside the wall. “Once upon a time” is the door. We step through together, and for a while, the isolation stops. Your loneliness and my loneliness, in the presence of a third thing—a hobbit’s quest, a princess’s curse, a detective’s puzzle—forget themselves. We become, briefly, a “we.”

My coffee is cold. I didn’t notice it happening. That’s the other thing about time: it steals temperature, light, love, people, right from under your nose while you’re busy narrating the theft.

I remember being nine years old and discovering that if I stared at a familiar word—say, “wall”—and repeated it aloud, it would dissolve into a meaningless sound. “Wall. Wall. Waaaalll.” It would become a strange, throaty chant, stripped of its purpose, just a vibration. I had found the secret: all meaning is a spell we agree to believe in. The world is just… stuff. We are the ones who name it, love it, fear it, paint it with associations. A rose is not inherently romantic. A crossroads is not inherently fateful. A house is not inherently a home. We pour those qualities into them, like water into a vase, and for a while, they hold the shape of our feelings.

Halfway through. Five hundred words used, five hundred to go. It feels like turning a ship in the open sea. You’ve left the sight of one shore, and the other isn’t yet visible. You’re just in the deep, blue, undifferentiated present.

I think about impermanence. The moss that quietly reclaims the steps. The way a friendship can fade not with a fight, but with a slowly accruing silence, a mutual, gentle laying-down of the shared world you’d built. The terrifying, beautiful truth that nothing is built to last, especially not us. Our bodies are in a constant state of decay and renewal, cells dying and being replaced so that we are, literally, not the same physical beings we were seven years ago. We are a pattern, a flame, not a statue. The goal isn’t to endure unchanged, but to burn clearly while we have the fuel.

We spend so much energy building bulwarks against that truth. We want the job to be permanent, the love to be eternal, the art to be immortal. But the art that moves us most is often about the ache of transience itself—the haiku, the fading photograph, the song that feels like a lost summer. We are moved by the beauty of the ruin, the elegance of the goodbye, the courage in the face of the ending. Perhaps we see our own reflection there.

A bird just landed on the windowsill, cocked its head, and flew off. A complete, perfect event. No prologue, no epilogue. It needed no words.

We, however, are cursed and blessed with the need to make it mean something. “A good omen,” we might think. Or, “It didn’t stay.” We cannot let a thing just be. We must translate it into the story of *us*.

Is that arrogance? Or is it the very essence of love? To pay such deep attention to the world that you weave it into your own narrative? To see the world not as a random assortment of objects, but as a participant in your life? The friendly cashier, the stubborn screw, the traffic jam that feels like a personal affront, the sunset that feels like a gift.

We are meaning-making machines. It’s our primary function. We take the raw data of existence and we spin a tale: “I am the hero.” “I am the victim.” “This is a tragedy.” “This is a comedy.” And the story becomes our reality. Change the story, and you change the life. It’s the oldest wisdom, dressed up as new self-help.

The cursor blinks. The word count climbs. The space is being filled, not with answers, but with the shape of a mind moving through an afternoon. This is all any of us can offer, really: not conclusions, but our witness. “I was here,” we say. “I saw the cobweb. I felt the cold coffee. I wondered about Lisbon and Kyoto and Toronto. I tried to connect. I failed beautifully. I tried again.”

And now, we are here, at the end. The thousand words are almost spent. The plain has been crossed. It wasn’t about arriving somewhere else; it was about the walking, the noticing, the tracing of a path that did not exist until it was walked.

The final words are always a surprise. You think you’re heading one place, and then the land turns, and you find yourself here.

Here. At a full stop. With the quiet returning. The hum, the distant siren, the empty cup. The web still trembles, empty. The page is no longer blank. It holds a record of a journey. It says, simply: someone passed this way. And for a little while, they were not alone. Because you, reading this, were with them. Two ghosts, humming through the wall. And for a moment, the wall vanished.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
